title: Gonalston Lancaster L7578 Memorial
cause_of_loss: Suspected parachute harness entangled in controls; aircraft dived from 8,000 ft
narrative: Lancaster L7578 was a historically significant aircraft — originally built as a Manchester bomber in 1940, later converted to a Lancaster. It had served with Squadrons 97, 207, 83, and Conversion Units 1654 and 1668 before arriving at 5 LFS. Notably, it was the aircraft used by Wing Commander John Nettleton VC for training prior to the famous Augsburg Raid. On 26 May 1944, P/O Sanderson's crew took off for a training exercise. The aircraft climbed to 8,000 feet, then suddenly dived into the ground at Gonalston. One theory suggests a parachute harness became entangled in the controls. All seven were buried together in a collective grave at Newark Cemetery. Eyewitnesses Arthur Knowles (later 91) and Kath Yates (85) attended a 75th anniversary memorial service in 2019, led by the Vice Lord-Lieutenant of Nottinghamshire and the Royal Canadian Air Force.

crew.name: George Benjamin Sanderson
crew.fate_detail: Killed when Lancaster L7578 dived from 8,000 feet into the ground at Gonalston. Sanderson was a 31-year-old Canadian, the eldest of the crew.
crew.bio: A 31-year-old Canadian pilot, Sanderson was the eldest member of the crew. He was partway through his Heavy Conversion Unit course, learning to fly four-engine bombers before posting to an operational squadron.
crew.burial_location: Newark Cemetery, Sec F, Collective Grave 309

crew.name: James Graham Middlemas
crew.fate_detail: Killed aged 19 in the crash at Gonalston. Buried with the entire crew in a collective grave at Newark Cemetery.
crew.burial_location: Newark Cemetery, Sec F, Collective Grave 309

crew.name: Albert Dennis Clark
crew.burial_location: Newark Cemetery, Sec F, Collective Grave 309

sources.title: Aircrew Remembered - P/O Sanderson, Lancaster L7578

maintainer.notes: Maintained by local community. Gonalston is a small parish with a parish meeting rather than a full council.

external_narratives.summary: Comprehensive page on P/O George Benjamin Sanderson and crew of Lancaster L7578 RC-L, 5 Lancaster Finishing School. The aircraft dived from 8,000 feet at 22:15 on 26 May 1944, crashing at Wood Barn near Gonalston. All seven crew killed.
external_narratives.key_details: Lancaster L7578 was historically significant, originally built as a Manchester in 1940 and converted. Used by Wing Commander John Nettleton VC for training before the Augsburg Raid. Possible cause was parachute harness entangled in controls.

external_narratives.summary: Imperial War Museum register entry for the Lancaster L7578 memorial at Gonalston. Stone memorial dedicated in 2010 commemorating all seven crew members.
external_narratives.key_details: Memorial reference 27204. Dedicated 2010. All seven crew buried in collective Grave 309, Section F, Newark Cemetery.

external_narratives.summary: Local history article about Lancaster L7578, detailing the crash at Gonalston and the aircraft's distinguished service record with multiple squadrons before arriving at 5 LFS Syerston.
external_narratives.key_details: Aircraft served with 97, 207, 83 Squadrons and Conversion Units 1654 and 1668 before 5 LFS. Eyewitnesses Arthur Knowles and Kath Yates attended the 75th anniversary service in 2019.

local_contacts.role: Local authority responsible for memorial upkeep
